title: Shimamura's Swimsuit
description: Adachi admires Shimamura's swimsuit—and envies how close it gets to her. At the pool, she finally works up the courage to hug her, skin to skin, and discovers what it means to be that close.
content:
When I looked closely, I realized the distance between Shimamura and her swimsuit was basically nonexistent. Obviously. Swimsuits were supposed to cling to the skin, after all. But seeing it up close made it more obvious: her swimsuit sat closer to her than any of her regular clothes ever could.

If I ever got that close… if I were pressed against her like that… my heart would probably burst on the spot. Just imagining it sent a bright, painful spark through my chest.

And that definitely wasn't because Shimamura was wearing a swimsuit.

Probably.

Maybe.

…Let's pretend that's not the reason.

If I had to explain it, the relationship between Shimamura and her swimsuit was kind of my ideal. That effortless closeness. That perfect fit. The way it wrapped around her—No, no, it wasn't about the parts it wrapped around. That wasn't the point. What I wanted was the zero-distance between them. Nothing in the way. No space at all.

I envied her swimsuit. What mine looked like didn't matter. What mattered was being able to cling to her the way her swimsuit did.

But no matter how much I wished for it, I couldn't turn into a swimsuit. Humans have limits. Of course… What was I even thinking? Maybe the heat had fried my brain. It felt like my skull was boiling. Wearing something so thin, with so much skin exposed, yet still feeling this hot—what was I supposed to do about that?

Well, putting that aside: I was wearing a swimsuit too. Way more skin out in the open than usual. Would Shimamura say anything about it?

I sipped my juice through the straw as noisily as possible, hoping she'd look at me. But she just stared straight up at the sky, muttering, 'So hot… it's so hot…' Worst case, even if she didn't compliment me, she could at least say something like, 'Your swimsuit is cute.' We'd gone to an indoor pool before—what had she said back then?

Standing around doing nothing felt pointless, so maybe I should actually try something.

There were dozens of things I wanted to do with Shimamura while we were both in swimsuits, but the moment I tried to start, my courage evaporated.

So I picked something simple. A swimsuit means… swimming, right?

I slipped into the water and started a slow, clumsy breaststroke. When I turned my head, Shimamura still had the straw between her lips, watching me. I kept my gaze on her as I swam. When I reached the pool wall, I pushed off and hurried back to her side.

…Nothing happened.

No, no, that wasn't right. That wasn't how this was supposed to go.

I thought hard, then forced my roundabout personality to walk in a straight line for once.

'Shimamura. Your swimsuit… it's cute.' Back straight, voice steady—I told her what had been playing on repeat inside my chest.

But really, why couldn't I say these things more casually? More brightly? Every compliment came out like I'd bumped into a wall and tripped. I wasn't even sure whether I was praising her or the swimsuit. No—that still wasn't direct enough. My real feelings, the shortest path between us, never fit inside words.

'…Really?' she asked.

'Y-Yes!' I nodded so hard I nearly toppled forward—then I hugged her tightly. Even here in the pool, hugging her felt no different from when we were on land.

'Shi—shubaa—!' I tried to call her name, but I overdid it, and the sound came out as some strange, garbled noise.

My jaw went slack. I felt like my soul was spilling out through my ears, my nose, my mouth—everywhere.

'Saba?' ('Mackerel?') Shimamura muttered.

Because that was when it hit me. It was different from hugging her on land.

Our skin was touching. Bare skin, with nothing at all in between. Waist, stomach—her stomach pressed right against mine. My skin hugged hers almost exactly the way her swimsuit did. Only the thinnest layer separated us. We were pressed together, close enough to merge.

I had become… the swimsuit.

My vision blurred. My head started spinning. A metallic clang went off inside my skull—like someone had smacked a giant gong right behind my eyes, the sound reverberating again and again.

It felt like I was teetering on the border between life and death, wrapped in some kind of blissfully numbing haze.

If I were ever destined to die, I had a feeling it would happen on a hot day just like this.
